data.title: Nebius Group: Too Little, To Late To Compete Successfully
data.description:
Executive Summary

The stock of Nebius Group (NASDAQ:NBIS), was an absolute sensation a few of months ago with the share price soaring from around $17 in mid-November to almost $50 by mid-February. This was mainly due to the overall post-election stock market craze and still robust sentiment around AI. However, the environment does not look that good at the moment. Moreover, the company operates in an extremely competitive environment. Nebius has some positives either, but its $5.5 billion valuation looks absolutely detached from reality as the company's TTM revenue is just $118 million. Due to massive overvaluation, I think that NBIS is an apparent Strong Sell.

Company Analysis

Nebius Group is a Dutch-based company that is involved in building a full-stack infrastructure for intensive AI workloads. The infrastructure includes large-scale GPU clusters, cloud platforms, and various tools for services and developers. Apart from the core platform, Nebius' ecosystem also includes Toloka AI (generative AI development), TripleTen (EdTech), and Avride (autonomous driving).

The company's ambitions to build a full-stack ecosystem covering potentially explosive industries are impressive. In theory, having a diverse set of offerings has the potential to cross-sell and increase switching costs for customers. I could have called Nebius an extremely interesting investment opportunity if the company was pioneering in all these niches.

However, the company will inevitably face fierce competition from American tech giants across its entire set of offerings. It is not secret, for instance, that Tesla has been investing billions in its autonomous driving (Full Self-Driving technology) for years. In addition, there is Waymo, Google's autonomous taxi service. Waymo is even ahead of Tesla in terms of generating value from shareholders by offering autonomous driving, as the service already operates in several large U.S. cities. There are also ambitious plans to deliver cutting-edge autonomous driving solutions from numerous Chinese EV players, including the world's largest EV maker. Therefore, it will be extremely challenging for NBIS to achieve success in this niche.

Trying to build a successful full-stack AI cloud platform will be even more challenging, as NBIS will face much more intense competition here. There are well-known cloud hyper-scalers, including AWS, Microsoft, and Google. Apart from them, there are several other large U.S. players, including Oracle ($350 billion market cap) and IBM ($204 billion market cap). All of these giants have vast financial resources and stellar success records. Meta and Palantir do not seem to plan to become full-stack AI ecosystems, but their footprint in AI is also expanding rapidly.

And the competition in AI is not limited to the U.S. market. In January 2025, we all saw an LLM from China, DeepSeek, spooked the entire U.S. technological sector. And DeepSeek is not a product from a technological giant, as it is owned by one of the Chinese startups. Apart from it, there are Chinese behemoths like Alibaba and Tencent. These giants should not be underestimated, as they both sit on a combined $100 billion+ cash pile, which is extremely likely to be mostly invested in AI. Alibaba is expected to release its flagship LLM this month, which will further elevate competition risks for NBIS.

In its latest presentation for investors, the management emphasized multiple times that the company boasts a $2 billion+ cash position. This is impressive only if we have nothing to compare. In a reality where U.S. tech giants plan to invest more than $300 billion in data centers in 2025, Nebius' cash position looks very small.

Valuation Is Too Generous

The valuation of Nebius is around $5.5 billion. Over the last twelve months, the company generated $117.5 million in sales. Therefore, the current TTM Price/Sales ratio is extremely high at almost 47. Wall Street analysts expect NBIS to deliver massive revenue growth in FY2025, which will significantly decrease the P/S ratio. Nevertheless, even with an expected 370% revenue growth in FY2025, the forward P/S ratio is still quite high at around 10. In addition, the company's EPS is expected to remain below zero in FY2025.

For a company without bottom-line profitability and very aggressive capital spending plans, it is very difficult to build a reliable DCF model. Nevertheless, this method seems the most reliable one for growth stocks. Before delving into FCF growth assumptions, I must calculate the cost of equity.

It is very tricky to forecast free cash flow growth for the company that is still yet to achieve bottom-line profitability. Moreover, recent developments demonstrate that the company aggressively invests in CAPEX. Current aggressive infrastructure spending is the reason why I did not add up the company's current $2.4 billion net cash position to the company's fair value.

We have year one revenue projection of $553 million, according to consensus. Let's assume a very aggressive 10% free cash flow margin for year 1, meaning $55.3 million in absolute terms. Let's also apply a super-aggressive 35.9% growth rate. The growth rate into perpetuity is also very optimistic at 7%.

With all these aggressive FCF growth assumptions, the company's fair value is 40% lower than its $5.5 billion market cap. To conclude, NBIS is massively overvalued.

Potential Drawbacks

Short-term share price movements are extremely unpredictable in the current environment. President Trump's moves in respect of tariffs look very chaotic, which significantly affects the stock market. Last week, we saw the real stock market panic after “Liberation Day”. And NBIS was no exception, as the stock demonstrated a massive share price plunge. On the other hand, there was an intraday 16% share price spike after the announcement of the higher tariff pause for most countries.

For such an extremely volatile stock like NBIS, short-term share price spikes might occur despite notable overvaluation. Therefore, there is a possibility of some short-term rallies, and this will make my bearish call look bad for a while. However, over the long term, the share price is highly likely to align with fundamentals.

Moreover, NBIS appears to be Wall Street's darling, as the stock has an average STRONG BUY rating with a quite high average target price of $55. On the other hand, Wall Street's target price has been quite volatile historically.

The Verdict

Nebius could have been an extremely interesting bet, deserving a premium over the fair value, if it was a first mover across all niches where it is developing its offerings. However, competing with American technological giants does not look like a promising endeavor.

data.title: How To Buy Low And Sell High Consistently For Lifelong Wealth
data.description:
Introduction:

Almost no one can always buy at the bottom and sell at the top. Not even the legends like Warren Buffett. To be clear, that should not be the goal either. The goal is to regularly buy in small lots when the prices of a particular stock are low (or undervalued) and sell in small lots when the prices appear to be overvalued. We are not trying to catch the bottom and top here, as there is no way to identify them in real-time. The strategy we are about to discuss is buying or selling small quantities of shares (of a stock) when certain predefined thresholds are met.

We want to acknowledge that we did not create this idea, as it is not something new and could be attributed to several past authors and books available in the public domain.

To be sure, this may not be an appropriate strategy for everyone. However, it may still be a very desirable strategy for conservative investors, especially those who are tired of frequent tantrums and downturns in the stock market, just like the one we are witnessing currently. This strategy is long-term and will help build wealth under most circumstances, be it a good market, a bad market, or an ugly one. It requires significant investment in the first few years, but afterward, it can run on the concept of selling something when it becomes too pricey and buying back when it becomes cheap again. Also, it works best with individual stocks that are blue-chip, dividend-paying, and have relatively stable fundamentals. It is not likely to work very well with broad indexes or ETFs. We suggest having diversification into a minimum of 15 companies.

In the end, we will also provide the results of a backtesting spanning nearly 30 years. The results are highly encouraging but should be viewed with a grain of salt. In the backtesting model, it is not possible to remove the selection bias entirely, though we have tried. Also, future markets can be different with entirely new trends, which may result in different outcomes.

Also, in brief, here are some caveats;

  • The title may seem a little ambitious, but this is not a get-rich-quick scheme. In practical terms, it is not an easy-to-follow strategy, especially for decades together. It is a very slow-moving strategy, as it involves very few trades in a given year. Many investors would leave it just out of boredom and lack of instant results.
  • The strategy would not work very well for lump sum investments. It requires capital inflows over several years.
  • The strategy needs constant work and some knowledge of the market. We still need to pick the right kind of stocks. However, if you pick a few bad apples along the way, it won't hurt the long-term results too much.

Note: We have written about this topic on SA a few times in the past. The last time we discussed this idea was two years ago. Every once in a while, we come back to re-evaluate the strategy by means of backtesting. This allows us and our regular readers to validate the process and its success. At the same time, it gives new readers fresh ideas and something to explore.

Buy-Low Sell-High Strategy ("BLSH") Using Blue-Chip DGI Stocks

Assumptions:

The strategy requires that you invest over time in small lots and not in one big lump sum. It suits the working folks perfectly because they earn a regular wage and generally invest (or can invest) a fixed sum of money at regular intervals. However, if you are retired, it may seem that the strategy would not work as all your money is already invested. But that's not wholly true. Retirees could also transition their portfolios to work on this strategy. They could earmark some of their holdings for this strategy and sell overvalued securities to raise some cash to be able to buy later. Since the strategy itself tells you when something is overvalued and when something is undervalued, there is no guesswork needed.

There are two parts to the strategy: the buy-strategy and the sell-strategy.

Buy Strategy:

We will select 15 stocks for our official portfolio. However, individual investors could select a minimum of 10 stocks or more. The idea is to have proper diversification. We use the following criteria to select the stocks:

  • They should be fairly large and stable companies with a market capitalization of at least $10 billion, preferably more.
  • All our companies would be dividend-paying companies. We should require that they at least have a 5-year dividend growth history (preferably 10 years).
  • We should also select some of the companies that have a history of doing relatively well during large corrections and recessions. However, this can change from event to event.

Target Buy Price:

For each stock in our portfolio, we will calculate the average of the past 252 trading days, which is generally equal to one year of trading data. Subsequent to this, we will multiply this average price by a 'Buy-Low Factor' to derive the target buy price. This factor is explained in the next paragraph. We can do this on a weekly basis or monthly basis. For the purpose of the backtesting presented in this article, we have used daily data (end-of-day data). Once the current price of a stock is below the 'target-buy-price,' that will signal to add/buy some shares. How many shares we buy will depend upon the total shares we can have for this stock and our current holding.

Buy-Low Factor:

This is something that we use for the sake of simplicity in implementation. We will use this factor to determine the suitable buy price for our next lot of shares for a particular stock. In other words, this is the price a stock must fall before it becomes eligible for buying more. The buy-low factor can generally vary between 0.85 and 0.90 depending upon the volatility of a stock.

For any given stock, we should look at the annual volatility. In our backtesting, we will only be using dividend-paying and stable blue-chip companies that we consider low-volatility stocks and the buy-low factor for such companies will be 0.90. For stocks with medium volatility, it should be modified to 0.85. We should preferably not consider very high volatility or non-dividend stocks for this strategy, but if we do, the Buy-Low-factor should be set accordingly, maybe 0.80 or lower.

You can obviously check the volatility of any stock (over a given period) and compare it with the S&P500. However, to keep things simple, you could also categorize stocks based on their past history and the industry they operate in and determine how volatile they are.

Some examples of low or high-volatility stocks:

  • Coca-Cola (KO), PepsiCo (PEP), or Proctor & Gamble (PG) can be considered as low volatility stocks.
  • Home Depot (HD) or Lowe's (LOW) would be considered medium volatility stocks.
  • Stocks like Microsoft (MSFT), Apple (AAPL), or Amgen (AMGN) could be considered slightly higher than medium.
  • Some examples of high-volatility stocks can be found in the tech sector. Many of them do not pay dividends, so they would not qualify for the test. Some examples of such companies are Meta (META) and Nvidia (NVDA). However, we have not tried this strategy with such stocks, and we feel it may not work well.

Nonetheless, for the purpose of this back-testing and for simplicity's sake, we will use the buy-low factor as 0.90 for all our stocks (that we use in backtesting)

Example:

Stock Ticker

Procter & Gamble

Volatility

Low

Buy-Low-factor

0.90

Initial shares to BUY

50 (the split-adjusted number could be a higher multiple)

Incremental shares to buy

50

Maximum holding for any stock

250-300 (in original shares, with counting for split shares or shares from reinvested dividends)

Maximum holding for any stock:

In our backtesting, we will put a maximum limit of 250 shares. This self-determined limit will define when a specific stock is eligible for a "sell" the next time the price reaches new highs. However, please note that this does not include shares acquired from the reinvested dividends or the increased share count due to splits.

Now, this limit can be adjusted (upwards or downwards) to suit your own portfolio size. For example, your total portfolio is $50,000, and you decide to divide it into 10 stocks. That means you can only assign $5,000 for one stock, and assuming the share price of the stock is $50, you could only have 100 shares of this stock. Once the holding reaches this level (of 100 shares), this stock will be eligible to sell in small fractions (20% at a time), and we could sell 20 shares when the prices reach a certain high threshold. Once it falls below 100 shares, we can buy 20 shares again if the prices reach a lower threshold. This will be more self-explanatory once you have read the entire article.

Sell-Strategy:

Sell-Strategy is, in fact, optional. If you are a buy-and-hold kind of investor, you may not sell at all. However, that would mean that you would need to constantly add new money to the portfolio to be able to take advantage of buying opportunities (when the price of an existing holding falls below a certain level).

That said, many investors would like to sell partially when a stock is overvalued and may buy it back when the price becomes attractive. This is the essence of the buy-low and sell-high strategy.

Target Sell Price:

Similar to the buying strategy, for each stock in our holdings, we will calculate the average price of the past 252 trading days. However, unlike the buy strategy, the sell-strategy will use multiple criteria before a sell decision is taken. This is to ensure that we do not end up selling too soon.

  • Current shares held match or exceed the threshold allocation (the threshold set for the maximum number of shares for any one stock). We can also set a dollar amount as a threshold.
  • The current price is at least > 1.15 times 252-days-average-price.
  • The current price is at least > 1.10 times the most recent buy-price (last-buy-price) for the stock.
  • The current price is at least > 1.50 times our average cost basis in the stock.

As you can see, the sell criteria are much more rigid compared to the buy strategy. The strategy will notify to sell only when all four conditions are met. We will generally sell a partial quantity. The number of shares that we will sell will usually be the same number of shares that we bought in the last buy transaction. The sell-strategy ensures two things: first, it will prevent us from getting overweight on any one stock. Secondly, it will ensure that we keep generating cash in the portfolio, which will enable buying the same or a different stock at a lower price or valuation in the future.

Example:

Stock Ticker

Procter & Gamble

Volatility

Low

Sale Target allocation:

250 Shares (max.)

200 Shares (min.)

So, we will sell only in partial lots (the limit of 250 shares does not include split shares or shares from the reinvested dividends).

Sell-high-factor:

There is not a single criterion but a combination of multiple criteria.

Number of shares to sell in one lot

50

Portfolio Selection and Backtesting:

In this strategy, stock selection is important and should not be taken lightly. However, we will keep selections limited to large-cap, solid blue-chip companies that also pay growing dividends. We also demand that these companies have paid and grown their dividends for a minimum of 5 years, but we would rather prefer a 10-year record.

Now, for back-testing purposes, we need to ensure that we remove the selection bias as much as possible. Obviously, it is almost impossible to remove bias completely, but we will consciously select some companies in the mix that have not performed well in the past, even though they would meet our selection criteria.

First, we will select some companies that not only meet our basic criteria but are also known to do okay during downturns and recessions. We will, of course, be careful to select companies from many different sectors and industry segments.

Some such companies are:

Automatic Data Processing (ADP), Amgen, Clorox (CLX), Canadian National Railway (CNI), Fastenal (FAST), Johnson & Johnson (JNJ), Lowe's, McDonald's (MCD), NextEra Energy (NEE), Procter & Gamble, Verizon (VZ), and Walmart (WMT).

As we stated before, to remove or negate the impact of selection bias, we will now intentionally select some companies that would have met most of our dividend criteria but still have not fared well in the last couple of decades. Three such stocks are Bank of America (BAC), Altria (MO), and Verizon (VZ). As such, all three companies have paid dividends for a long time, but their performances have been quite unattractive.

BAC performed very badly in the 2008-2009 period due to the subprime mortgage crisis. BAC also reduced its dividend from 32 cents to just 1 cent a quarter during that time. The company eventually survived and recovered, but its share price still trades below its pre-recession levels.

The second company that has performed badly during the last several years is Altria, though it was a top performer earlier. VZ is an example of a moderate performer, but in the last couple of years, it has underperformed the broader market significantly due to concerns about its large debt load. Besides, we have also included some other moderate performers like Walmart.

Our back-testing model selects 15 companies and keeps the same stocks for the entire period of roughly 30 years. However, in real life, we could add or remove some stocks when conditions and ground realities change, though it should not be frequent.

Table-1: The final list of 15 stocks:

Backtesting: Buy Low and Sell High Strategy

For backtesting purposes, we will make the following assumptions:

  • The backtesting period will be from Jan. 1995 until April 04, 2025, roughly a period of 30 years.
  • For any one stock, we will buy a maximum of 250–300 shares; however, only 50 shares at a time.
  • We will initiate the sell-strategy only when the holding (for any stock) is 250 or more. No sale will occur when the shares count falls to 200 or below. We can put additional conditions based on invested capital; for example, we sell only when the actual invested capital in the stock has exceeded $25,000.
  • The backtesting will cover a period from January 1995 until April 04, 2025.

Buy rules:

  • We buy the first 50 shares of each of the 15 stocks on the first day of the testing period. That is our first and only investment without any system-generated buy signal. Please note that the number of shares is assumed to be on a pre-split basis. For example, let's say we bought 50 shares in Jan. 1995, which was followed by two 2:1 splits, first in 1997 and second in 2004. In this case, post-split (s), our original 50 shares would become 200 (50*2*2) shares.
  • Buy any subsequent shares when the current price is less than the Buy-Low factor (0.90 for stocks in our selection) of the average price of the past year (roughly equivalent to 252 trading days).
  • Also, after our first buy for a stock, any subsequent purchase will be at least 252 trading days apart.

Sell rules:

  • Check the shares count threshold (as well as the dollar amount). No sell signal is generated unless the total shares in a company have reached 250 or the invested capital in the stock has exceeded $25,000, excluding the reinvested dividends.
  • Sell if the current price is > 1.15 times the average price of the past year (that is 252 trading days) and > 1.10 times the price of the last (most recent) purchase, and > 1.50 times the average purchase price of the stock in our portfolio.
  • Any two subsequent sales will be at least 252 trading days apart.

In our backtesting, we used the daily EOD (end-of-day) data, and the system ran the check to generate buy or sell signals based on our rules.

Below, we present tables (covering each of the 15 stocks) covering buy-sell transactions, which were determined by our system during the period from 01/01/1995 until 04/04/2025.

Prices shown (in the tables) are split-adjusted prices.

Note: Column "Split-Ratio" - If there were two splits of 2:1 after the purchase date, the shares prior to two splits will be considered to have a split ratio of 4:1 (2*2:1), and so on. The total shares shown are on a pre-split basis.

Table-2-a: ADP

Table-2-b: AMGN

Table-2-c: BAC

Table-2-d: CLX

Table-2-e: CNI

Table-2-f: FAST

Table-2-g: JNJ

Table-2-h: LOW

Table-2-i: MCD

Table-2-j: MO

Table-2-k: NEE

Table-2-l: PG

Table-2-m: TXN

Table-2-n: VZ

Table-2-o: WMT

Model Portfolio and Performance :

Below is the portfolio with its current value, cost basis, and the adjusted cost basis. The cost basis is calculated by taking into account the actual capital spent to buy the shares without accounting for re-invested dividends. However, the "adjusted-cost-basis" is calculated from the original cost basis minus any capital (including profit/loss) received from the sales of a partial lot of shares during the period.

As you can see from table-3A, it provides the resultant portfolio if the transactions of 15 stocks are put together in a portfolio manager and dividends are allocated and reinvested. Some stocks have given outsized returns, while others have provided mediocre or lower returns. Different stocks will give different results during a particular period, and that's what we should expect. But there will be some that would perform well below expectations.

The model portfolio grew into a large sum of nearly $3.6 million in 30 years, providing a CAGR of roughly 14.40% (with dividends reinvested), compared to 10.3% of the buy-and-hold S&P 500. The results were even better prior to the start of the current market downturn.

Table-3A: Portfolio Value

Below, we present the performance comparison of the model BLSH portfolio (Buy Low Sell High) with the S&P 500.

Table – 3B:

As you can see in the above table, the last three columns for S&P500 are not much different, though there are slight variations. Column 3 is calculated as if similar amounts were invested on the same dates as the model portfolio investments were made. Column 4 is calculated using the BLSH model strategy with SPY as the only asset. The last column shows if the lump sum investments were made at the beginning of the period. This shows that the S&P500 would not get any alpha by using the BLSH strategy. The same would likely apply to any other broad ETF or fund.

However, a diversified portfolio of 15 stocks with this strategy can provide substantial alpha over a long period of time, especially with very small initial capital actually invested in the market.

Amounts invested over the years in the 15-stock portfolio:

The table below shows the yearly transaction amounts (of purchases and sales) and the net investment each year.

Table-3C:

In this model, with the automated (system-guided) strategy, we have invested $510,935 over the years but have sold stocks worth $443,916 as well. This works out to a net investment capital of $67,019 invested over the years. However, it should be noted that in the first few years, we will see larger capital inflows due to the fact that the strategy does not sell until a threshold is reached. After the first 6–8 years, the capital inflow requirements subside greatly due to the fact that we almost sell as much as we buy.

Concluding Thoughts

As stated earlier, there is no crystal ball here. However, this is a systematic and methodical approach that eliminates the guesswork and emotions from your trading. It also demands that you stick to relatively safe and blue-chip stocks. The strategy results in superior returns over a long period of time. Another big advantage (which can be a minus for some) is that this portfolio does not require the entire investment upfront; rather, it allows us to invest gradually over many years. Once we start selling partial lots, our initial investment gradually reduces to insignificant levels. Our backtesting model demonstrated that even if we were to make a few choices in terms of picking the stocks (stocks that performed subpar or poorly), overall results would still have been outstanding.

The idea here is to provide a basic framework of the strategy to our readers so they can fine-tune it according to their individual needs. We recommend you do further research and due diligence before diving into any such strategy.

data.title: President Trump Hands A Big Gift To REIT Investors
data.description:
President Trump is famous for being a real estate mogul.

He is reported to have made billions of dollars in real estate investments, and to this day, a very substantial portion of his wealth is invested in a vast portfolio of properties ranging from skyscrapers in Manhattan to hotels in Las Vegas and even golf courses in Scotland.

But he has also suffered his fair share of setbacks, with many of his businesses going bankrupt and himself nearly losing it all during the great financial crisis because he was over-leveraged and couldn't repay his debt. Therefore, he is very aware of the danger that debt can present to real estate investors, particularly in a rising interest rate environment.

This brings me to today.

There are growing rumors that he might be intentionally trying to crash the economy in order to bring interest rates to lower levels. He himself just shared a post on Truth Social (DJT) that implied that this was his intention to bring interest rates to lower levels. He has also made many public calls to the Fed Chair Jerome Powell to cut interest rates to stimulate the economy. The last one just a few days ago:

It would save the country a lot of money as it will need to refinance $9 trillion worth of treasuries this year alone, and it would, of course, also benefit him personally since he is mostly invested in real estate.

And if that was truly his plan, it seems to be working.

The market is in a panic that we could be headed into a recession as a result of his trade war escalation, and it has already pushed long-term interest rates to much lower levels.

Earlier this year, the 10-year treasury (US10Y) was at nearly 5%. It is below 4% as I write this:

Who benefits the most from such a steep drop in interest rates?

Real estate and REIT investors (VNQ) benefit.

It is a real gift to them, as they have suffered for years due to the rising interest rates.

It has put a lot of stress on real estate markets as over-leveraged landlords had to refinance mortgages at higher interest rates, reducing profits, and in some cases, faced defaults as they were unable to refinance. It has also hurt property levels as cap rates expanded.

As a result of this, publicly listed REITs have suffered a three-year-long bear market. Private landlords like Trump also suffered and would be very happy to see a return to lower interest rates.

However, the steep drop in interest rates is now bringing new hope to the sector.

Historically, REITs have strongly outperformed in declining interest rate environments, and it appears that this is where we are headed.

As a result of President Trump's latest actions, the debt market is now pricing a 99.4% chance of at least one rate cut by June 18th:

And by the end of this year, the most likely outcome is that interest rates will be a full 100 basis points lower as the Fed steps in to stimulate the economy as it faces growing pains from the trade war:

This would be very good news for REIT investors.

As a reminder, REITs have seen their share prices crash, and this is entirely because of the surge in interest rates.

Therefore, it would seem logical for them to recover as interest rates are cut down to lower levels.

The negative correlation should hold both ways around:

It also makes sense when you think of it.

REITs crashed when rates surged because investors could suddenly earn a decent yield from bonds and treasuries. A lot of investors used to own REITs as bond replacements in a low-yield world, but they had no real interest in owning REITs in the long run.

However, those same investors are likely to come running back to the REIT sector as rates are cut and bonds become less desirable.

There is a big difference between being able to earn a 5% yield versus only earning a 3% yield from Treasuries. This is particularly true if inflation now accelerates, resulting in even lower real yields after inflation.

REITs, on the other hand, are offering exceptional value and high yields right now after their 3-year-long bear market. Their valuations are quite literally at a 10-year low, with many high-quality REITs offering 6-10% dividend yields:

Here are a few examples:

VICI Properties Inc. (VICI) is the leading casino net lease REIT in the world. It owns some of the best Class A assets in the world, such as the Caesars Palace, and it leases them on a triple-net basis with multi-decade long leases that include annual rent escalations (tied to CPI) and put all property expenses, including even the maintenance, on the tenant. It has consistently managed to grow its cash flow per share, even through the pandemic, at an 8% historic average since it went public. Yet, it is today offering at a near 6% dividend yield, and they are in a good position to keep hiking this dividend. President Trump also owns a major investment in the Las Vegas strip, with a 50% equity stake in the Trump International Hotel.

Healthcare Realty Trust Incorporated (HR) is one of the biggest REITs focusing on medical office buildings. This property sector is undersupplied and benefits from growing demand as a result of the rapidly aging population. This year, it has guided its same property NOI to grow by about 3% again. The property sector is recession-resistant and should not be severely impacted by the tariffs. Yet, the REIT is offering at a historically low valuation and yields 8% at the moment:

Armada Hoffler Properties, Inc. (AHH) is a smaller REIT that owns mostly mixed-use properties, combining residential, retail, and office under one roof. The REIT has suffered in recent years because residential and offices were oversupplied and faced stagnating rents. However, a benefit of the tariffs is that they will greatly increase the cost of construction, thereby reducing future supply and increasing future rent growth. It also increases the replacement value of existing constructions. We expect rent growth to accelerate in the coming years, particularly for residential properties, and yet, the REIT is now priced at its lowest valuation ever, trading at a 12% cash flow yield, out of which it distributes an 8% dividend yield and reinvests the rest in growth.

Closing Note

President Trump just gave a huge gift to REIT investors.

He is a major real estate investor, so this may not be a complete coincidence.

On the flip side, most other sectors of the market may suffer as tariffs go into effect, increasing the cost of imports and facing retaliation from other countries.

We are happy to be heavily invested in REITs in this uncertain environment and expect them to benefit relative to the rest of the market.

data.title: 7 Quality Dividend Stocks I'm Buying As Tariff Risks Remain
data.description:
Whew!

That was a close one.

The world pulled back from the brink last Wednesday as the Arsonist/Firefighter-in-Chief issued a 90-day pause on most countries' tariffs in the latest episode of the trade war soap opera. But the truth is that tariff risks remain, which is why the massive relief rally on Wednesday gave way to a reflexive selloff on Thursday (the day of this writing).

Despite a softer-than-expected March CPI report, showing headline CPI climbing "only" 2.4% year-over-year primarily as a result of a ~10% YoY drop in gasoline, the tariffs still in place (10% universal tariff plus 145% on China and 25% on autos, steel, and aluminum) will almost certainly lead to a rebound in inflation over the course of this year.

Citi analysis indicates that these tariffs alone push up the average effective tariff rate above 20%.

On the other hand, one cannot ignore the disinflationary forces at play, such as weaker economic growth, lower oil prices, and the inevitable decline in the shelter component of the CPI over the next year or so.

Even if the tariffs don't change again at all going forward, "real damage has already been done to the economy," and the China trade war remains a major risk, says Dan Ives.

But given the fact that tariffs have only been paused for this 90-day window as well as the fact that well-thought-out trade deals with dozens if not hundreds of countries will take much longer than that to work out, and given that Trump's instincts remain protectionist, trade-related risks have by no means been put to rest.

Here's what's on the docket for this week:

  • A deep dive inside the tariff soap opera, Trump's trade philosophy, why uncertainty is bound to continue, and why recession odds remain elevated.
  • Why I think the time to raise cash is now in the rearview mirror.
  • Two REITs I own but currently lack the conviction to buy more of.
  • My buy list of dividend growth stocks that, I believe, should be able to navigate any storm ahead.

Onward!

Inside The Tariff Drama

As I discussed in "6 Dividend Growth Stocks I'm Buying As Tariffs Crush The Economy," there is a lot of ideological imputation done to President Trump.

Since the president is an instinctive negotiator as well as a longtime protectionist, there is enough space for almost anyone to project their own trade philosophy onto him and think that he believes exactly what they do—that his goals are exactly what theirs are.

Some see Trump's ultimate goal as "fair trade," and the presence of people like Scott Bessent in his administration lends credence to this notion. Others see Trump's ultimate goal as a tariff-fueled US manufacturing boom, and the presence of people like Peter Navarro in his administration lends credence to this idea.

My own view, as I've written many times, is that Trump is a thoroughgoing protectionist who believes that it would be good to substitute imports for domestic production. Trump views all outsourcing of production as theft of American wealth. It also seems clear to me that Trump views trade deficits as an indication of outsourcing and therefore US manufacturing job losses... and therefore theft of American wealth.

This is a rehash of the 400-year-old mercantilist idea that exports are good and make a nation richer, while imports are bad and make a nation poorer.

During his first term, Trump wrote in the margins of a rough draft speech, "Trade is bad."

Trump's experience in the dog-eat-dog world of New York real estate seems to have reinforced his belief that international relations are a zero-sum transaction with a winner and loser. Foreign countries have been winning, and the US has been losing.

Recently, Trump told reporters on Air Force One: "To me, a [trade] deficit is a loss. We're gonna have surpluses or at worst we're gonna be breaking even."

He said this in the context of other countries approaching him about a trade deal, which indicates to my mind that the only trade deals Trump will agree to are ones that he perceives will eliminate the trade deficit.

Hence, his strident assertion that these tariffs are a permanent policy and his repeated claim that manufacturers are stampeding into the US.

After the “Liberation Day” announcement, a White House official explained the administration's view that "the trade deficit that we have with any given country is the sum of all unfair trade practices, the sum of all cheating."

So, what changed the president's mind?

Was it the ~$10 trillion of market cap wiped out in the stock market?

Perhaps in part, but there were two other deciding factors.

First, on Wednesday, the Wall Street Journal reported that the president watched the Fox Business interview with JP Morgan (JPM) CEO Jamie Dimon, in which Dimon said that recession is a likely outcome from the tariffs and urged the president to give Bessent time to negotiate trade deals.

Then, the WSJ reported on Thursday that Treasury Secretary Bessent flew down to Florida over the previous weekend to persuade the president to focus on negotiations instead of a hardline stance. This resulted in Bessent being granted additional authority on this matter.

Finally, on Wednesday, with Bessent and Commerce Secretary Howard Lutnick flanking him in the Oval Office (and Peter Navarro conspicuously absent), the president decided to grant the 90-day pause.

At this point, while there is some relief in the short term, there is no greater clarity or certainty about long-term policy from the White House. On one hand, Trump hasn't ruled out extending the 90-day pause. On the other hand, Trump also says that the “Liberation Day” tariffs could go back into effect if countries don't agree to trade deals by the end of the 90 days.

What kind of slapdash trade deals can the administration throw together in the next three months? We'll see.

Again, though, given Trump's idiosyncratic instincts, the only “fair” trade deal between countries is one that eliminates the trade deficit.

Furthermore, given the way the “Liberation Day” tariff rates were calculated, it seems that equal trade in physical goods between countries is what Trump deems "fair."

This, of course, demonstrates an ignorance of the fact that those US dollars flowing into foreign countries as a result of goods trade deficits return to the US in the form of exported US services and foreign investment into US capital markets.

As the WSJ puts it:

For decades, the U.S. and the rest of the world had a deal: Other countries sent cars, phones, clothes and food to the U.S., and in return they got bonds, software and management consultants.

It's a great deal for the US. We export high-margin services while importing low-margin goods. That results in the creation of high-paying jobs in services sectors, while low-paying goods-producing jobs go elsewhere.

That's how comparative advantage works.

American businesses export over $1 trillion per year in services, including tourism but also, more importantly, software-as-a-service (“SaaS”).

A huge portion of American software companies' revenue comes from international markets.

That makes the current trade war a big risk for Big Tech companies.

If Trump insists upon balancing trade in physical goods between countries, what effect does that have on foreigners' purchase of US SaaS?

Will foreign companies shift away from using the cloud storage of Microsoft Azure (MSFT), Amazon Web Services (AMZN), or Google Cloud (GOOG, GOOGL) in favor of a domestic cloud provider? Will they ditch Intuit's QuickBooks (INTU) in exchange for non-US bookkeeping software? Will they stop using ChatGPT or Google Gemini in exchange for a local or regional AI chatbot?

And if the flow of USD into foreign countries ceases, then presumably foreign investment into US financial assets will likewise cease.

Foreigners hold some 20% of all US equities, 30% of US Treasuries, and 30% of US corporate debt.

This poses a major risk to both the stock and credit markets that I think investors are beginning to grasp.

If foreigners begin selling en masse, it could cause an extended downturn in both stock and bond prices.

Indeed, we have already been seeing this, as foreign selling of US stocks has reached feverishly high levels.

As a result of the bad will toward the US around the world, foreign investors have discovered both personal and financial reasons to reduce their exposure to US financial assets recently.

It is a relief that Trump can be convinced to keep pausing the worst of his tariffs, but can he be dissuaded from his decades-long belief in autarky and mercantilism?

In June 1930, an executive at JP Morgan Bank named Thomas Lamont visited President Herbert Hoover in his office to plead against tariffs. Lamont later recalled, "I almost went down on my knees to beg Herbert Hoover to veto the asinine Hawley-Smoot tariff. That act intensified nationalism all over the world." It didn't work. Hoover signed the bill into law anyway.

Could Jamie Dimon, Lamont's modern-day successor, dissuade Trump from tariffs? Or would he be no more successful than Lamont was in 1930?

Expect volatility to continue because the market has nothing akin to clarity or certainty about the long-term trade policy outlook. Investors should expect tariffs to remain a Damocles Sword hanging over our heads for the next four years.

Interestingly, recession odds on betting site Kalshi have only slightly declined in the wake of the 90-day pause.

Polymarket's equivalent currently (as of this writing) puts the odds of a 2025 recession a little lower, at 58%.

What This Dividend Growth Investor Is Buying Now

The best thing to have going into a bear market is cash.

The second-best thing to have going into a bear market is cash flow in the form of dividends.

Today, with the S&P 500 (SPY) ~15% off its high and lots of dividend stocks going on sale, I would argue that the time has passed to raise cash. The time to do that was a month or more ago. Now, I would argue the best thing for investors to do is to steadily deploy whatever cash they have into the best opportunities before them.

"Be greedy when others are fearful," as the Buffett saying goes.

Alas, almost systematically, cash levels rise as stocks sell off. See, for example, the surge in money market AUM that coincided with the beginning of the S&P 500 selloff:

But if you zoom out further, you will find that MMF AUM always explodes higher during market panics and selloffs, such as the pandemic and the Great Recession:

The thinking always seems to be, "I'll cut my losses now to raise cash for the really good deals that will come later."

But how do you know you aren't selling at or near the bottom? Especially since there is $7 trillion in dry powder sitting on the sidelines that could be deployed into stocks at any moment?

I lack the omniscience or luck required to perfectly time the bottom, so instead I choose simply to keep buying on the way down. If a stock I've deemed a good value goes lower in price and becomes an even better value, I'll buy more of it and lower my cost basis.

Hence, my rule to "always be buying something". That goes for good times and bad times, bull markets and bear markets.

That doesn't mean I won't sometimes raise cash when good stock-buying opportunities are limited, but very rarely have I ever found no reasonably valued buying opportunities at all. (Late 2021 was one of those rare periods.)

In addition to my "always be buying something" rule, I mentioned in last week's article my rule to double down on quality during market downturns.

I'll add one more rule to the list:

  • When dry powder is limited (and when is it not?), focus on your highest conviction and highest confidence picks.

Two REITs in particular would both qualify as “high-quality” in my opinion, and both look ultra-cheap, but certain major unknowns prevent me from gaining the conviction to buy more right now.

One of those is Alexandria Real Estate Equities (ARE), owner and developer of Class A life science centers.

Firstly, I simply cannot determine at this time whether they have been or will be affected by NIH or FDA lease cancellations. I know that the NIH has a significant presence at their campus in Rockville, Maryland, but I don't know whether this lease was canceled or not.

Secondly, and perhaps more importantly, I don't know what effect the layoff of thousands of FDA employees, including many involved in the drug approval process, will have on biopharma tenants' R&D spending plans. A Barron's article asserts that the FDA layoffs will slow drug approvals. How much will this diminish demand for life science space?

I don't know. Therefore, I lack the conviction to buy ARE, despite its 7%+ dividend yield and ~10x AFFO multiple.

The other high-quality yet cheap REIT I lack the conviction to buy is Rexford Industrial Realty (REXR), owner of Class A infill industrial properties in Southern California.

REXR's management insists that their portfolio isn't directly affected by trade volumes at the two critically important SoCal ports in Los Angeles and Long Beach, but the whole SoCal economy is certainly affected by activity at these ports. The Port of LA expects a 10% decline in inbound cargo from China this year, while the Port of Long Beach expects a 20% decline.

However, this analysis was done before the most recent ramp-up in trade tensions between the US and China. Trade volumes could plunge even more than 10-20% at SoCal's ports.

That will have ripple effects on SoCal's economy.

If I believed that this trade war will resolve quickly with a new trade deal between the two countries, I might have the conviction to buy REXR. But right now, it seems entirely possible that the US and China cut off the majority of trade with each other in the coming months, which would ultimately reduce tenant demand for space at REXR's properties.

Then again, at some point Trump could have a “beautiful call” with Xi Jinping and agree to some agreement that he thinks will shrink the trade deficit, in which case REXR would likely rally.

Unfortunately, I lack the conviction that that will happen.

Instead, my 7-stock buy list is made up of dividend stalwarts that, I think, should do at least okay under almost any circumstance.

Dividend Yield Projected Dividend Growth Rate (Guesstimate)
American Homes 4 Rent (AMH) 3.5% Mid- to High-Single-Digit
Blackstone (BX) ~4% Variable (Avg. High-Single-Digit)
EastGroup Properties (EGP) 3.7% High-Single-Digit
Alphabet (GOOGL) 0.5% Double Digit
Hannon Armstrong Sustainable Infrastructure (HASI) 7.4% Mid-Single-Digit
Public Storage (PSA) 4.3% Mid-Single-Digit
Xcel Energy (XEL) 3.3% Mid-Single-Digit

American Homes 4 Rent

Single-family rental REIT AMH's two primary vulnerabilities to the trade war are (1) input costs for the ~2,000 new homes it builds annually and (2) economic weakness that could lead to tenant defaults.

But the 2,000 annual new-build homes are a small fraction of AMH's ~60,000 home portfolio, and it would take quite a bit of economic weakness for a critical mass of tenants to default on their leases or forsake single-family homes for the apartment life.

Blackstone

BX may actually be a net beneficiary of economic weakness, at it has lots of dry powder in its various funds to opportunistically invest.

EastGroup Properties

EGP has some exposure to Southern California, but most of its infill industrial space is in Sunbelt states like Texas, Florida, and North Carolina that are still experiencing strong population growth.

Alphabet

As stated above, GOOGL's biggest threat right now might just be a foreign shift away from US SaaS providers like Google Cloud. But so far, American tech companies have mostly been spared foreign retaliation, and I think it will stay that way. After all, Google is not as tied to the president as is, say, Tesla (TSLA).

Hannon Armstrong Sustainable Infrastructure

HASI's financial investments in the green energy space do not appear to be under threat by the recent budget bill in Congress right now, primarily because four GOP senators have voiced their opposition to repealing the tax credits for renewable energy. Even if those tax credits did get repealed, it would likely have only a minimal impact on HASI's portfolio, which is mostly in the form of credit and preferred equity investments.

Public Storage

In a roundabout way, steel and aluminum tariffs have a positive effect on existing self-storage real estate because they make construction of new self-storage properties (that use a heckuva lot of steel) a lot more expensive. Weaker economic growth or a recession would offset the benefits of higher barriers to entry (via higher construction costs), but self-storage does tend to be one of the most recession-resistant forms of real estate.

Xcel Energy

I don't own XEL yet, but I've had my eye on this high-quality utility company for a while. It operates in several Northern states, Colorado, and a region of Texas and New Mexico.

The company has a long and consistent track record of EPS growth, which has facilitated over 20 years of consecutive dividend growth of about 5% per year. It boasts a strong balance sheet, a fairly low 60% payout ratio, low customer bills, and ample demand from data centers.

XEL is as unsexy as it gets, but this dividend growth stalwart provides a nice, all-weather steadiness to an investment portfolio.

data.title: Weekly Indicators: Short Leading Indicators Turn Negative Despite Whipsaw In Markets
data.description:
Purpose

I look at the high frequency weekly indicators because while they can be very noisy, they provide a good nowcast of the economy, and will telegraph the maintenance or change in the economy well before monthly or quarterly data is available. They are also an excellent way to "mark your beliefs to market." In general, I go in order of long leading indicators, then short leading indicators, then coincident indicators.

A Note on Methodology

Data is presented in a "just the facts, ma'am" format with a minimum of commentary so that bias is minimized.

Where relevant, I include 12-month highs and lows in the data in parentheses to the right. All data taken from St. Louis FRED unless otherwise linked.

A few items (e.g., Financial Conditions indexes, regional Fed indexes, stock prices, the yield curve) have their own metrics based on long-term studies of their behavior.

Where data is seasonally adjusted, generally it is scored positively if it is within the top 1/3 of that range, negative in the bottom 1/3, and neutral in between. Where it is not seasonally adjusted, and there are seasonal issues, waiting for the YoY change to change sign will lag the turning point. Thus I make use of a convention: data is scored neutral if it is less than 1/2 as positive/negative as at its 12-month extreme.

With long leading indicators, which by definition turn at least 12 months before a turning point in the economy as a whole, there is an additional rule: data is automatically negative if, during an expansion, it has not made a new peak in the past year, with the sole exception that it is scored neutral if it is moving in the right direction and is close to making a new high.

For all series where a graph is available, I have provided a link to where the relevant graph can be found.

Recap of Monthly Reports

March data included the best consumer and producer price reports since May 2020, but the worst consumer confidence as measured by the U. of Michigan in over 40 years.

Long leading indicators

Interest rates and credit spreads

Rates

  • BAA corporate bond index 6.37%, up +0.42% w/w (1-yr range: 5.33-6.37) (new 12 month high)
  • 10-year Treasury bonds 4.49%, up +0.49% w/w (3.66-4.98)
  • Credit spread 1.88%, down -0.07% w/w (1.36-2.42).

(Graph at Moody's Seasoned Baa Corporate Bond Yield | FRED | St. Louis Fed.)

Yield curve

  • 10 year minus 2 year: +0.52%, up +0.16% w/w (-1.07 - +0.52) (new 12 month high)
  • 10 year minus 3 month: +0.16%, up +0.43% w/w (-1.65 - 0.17)
  • 2 year minus Fed funds: -0.36%, up +0.33% w/w.

(Graph at 10-Year Treasury Constant Maturity Minus 2-Year Treasury Constant Maturity | FRED | St. Louis Fed.)

30-Year conventional mortgage rate (from Mortgage News Daily)

  • 7.07%, up +0.47% w/w (6.11-7.52).

Treasury yields rose sharply, but are still significantly below their 12-month high of 2 months ago. Corporate bond yields did make a 12 month high, as did high yield bonds, indicating investors are demanding increasing protection for taking risks. Mortgage rates also increased significantly, but are also below their 12-month highs. Both, as well as mortgage rates, have since backed off. The bottom line is that higher long rates are a negative.

Housing

Mortgage applications (from the Mortgage Bankers Association)

  • Purchase apps up +9% to 157 (114-163) [SA]
  • Purchase apps 4 wk avg. up 2 to 147 [SA]
  • Purchase apps YoY% up +15% [NSA]
  • Purchase apps YoY 4 wk avg. +11.5% [NSA]
  • Refi apps up +20% w/w [SA]
  • Refi apps YoY up +93% [SA].

*[SA] = seasonally adjusted, [NSA] = not seasonally adjusted.

Real Estate Loans (from the FRB)

  • Down -0.1% w/w
  • Up +1.2% YoY (tied for post-pandemic low).

(Graph at Real Estate Loans, All Commercial Banks | FRED | St. Louis Fed.)

Both purchase mortgage and refinancing applications have been volatile, but within a limited range over the past 18 months. Both are now higher YoY, but are close to their longer-term bottoms, so they score neutral.

In 2023 real estate loans sank below 1/2 of their 12-month-high, the last housing indicator to turn negative, and have generally continued to worsen. They paused for several months, then in the last several months made several new YoY lows, including this week.

Money supply

The Federal Reserve has discontinued this weekly series. Data is now only released monthly. February data was just released two weeks ago:

  • M1 m/m up +0.4%, YoY Real M1 up +0.2% (2.5 year high) (worst: -1.4% 8/23)
  • M2 m/m up +0.4%, YoY Real M2 up +1.1% (2.5 year high) (worst -8.6% 9/23).

No recession has happened without a YoY real M1 negative, or YoY real M2 below +2.5%. Real M2 fell below that threshold in March 2022. Real M1 also turned negative as of May 2022. Two months real M1 finally breached that threshold to the upside, but sank back below zero, and back to negativity, last month. This month it is positive again.

Real M2 remains within 0.1% of its “least bad” reading since early 2022. In the past, when either has turned positive YoY, that was near either the middle or end of recessions, which warrants a change to positive.

Corporate profits (Q4 actual + Q1 estimated) from I/B/E/S via FactSet) at p. 30

  • Q4 actual unchanged w/w at 65.77, up +4.7% q/q
  • Q1 estimated down -0.20 to 60.05, down -8.7% q/q.

FactSet estimates earnings, which are replaced by actual earnings as they are reported, and are updated weekly. The "neutral" band is +/-3%. I also average the previous two quarters together, until at least 100 companies have actually reported. The average of Q4 and Q1 earnings are -2.0%, so this metric has changed from positive to neutral.

Credit conditions (from the Chicago Fed)

  • Financial Conditions Index up +0.03 (less loose) to -0.54 (-0.32 - -0.67)
  • Adjusted Index (removing background economic conditions) down -0.02 (looser) to -0.61 (-0.26 - -0.69)
  • Leverage subindex up +0.08 (less loose) to -0.61 (+0.05 - -0.76).

In these indexes, lower = better for the economy. The Chicago Fed's Adjusted Index's real break-even point is roughly -0.25. In the leverage index, a negative number is good, a positive poor. The historical breakeven point has been -0.5 for the unadjusted Index. Recently, this Index went below -0.60, warranting a rating change to positive, but last week retreated back into the neutral range. The leverage subindex is also positive for the economy.

Short leading indicators

Economic Indicators from the late Jeff Miller’s “Weighing the Week Ahead”

  • Miller Score (formerly "C-Score"): down -17 to 350, down -2 m/m (209 5/24/24 - 367 3/21/25)
  • St. Louis Fed Financial Stress Index: up +0.7260 to +0.0944 (+0.0944 4/21/25 - -.9762 1/31/25) (new 12 month high)
  • BCIp from Georg Vrba: down -5.4 to +48.3 as of 4/3/25 iM's Business Cycle Index (100 is max value, below 25 is a recession signal averaging 20 weeks ahead).

The Miller Score is designed to look 52 weeks ahead for whether or not a recession is possible. Any score over 500 means no recession. This number fell below that threshold at the beginning of August 2021, so not only is it negative, but we are now well into the “recession eligible” time period. This indicator is heavily influenced by both the yield curve and the still very low unemployment rate vs. gas prices(expecting a reversal) which explains why it remains negative. I am exploring disentangling the two components with the forecaster who authors this score to see if a better signal can be derived.

The St. Louis Financial Stress index is one where a negative score is a positive for the economy, and during its limited existence, has risen above zero before a recession by less than one year. It remained very positive - until this week, when it recorded its worst reading since the 2023 collapse of Silicon Valley Bank.

Since last April IM has stated that their system does not forecast a recession.

Trade weighted US$

  • Down -0.59 to 126.07 w/w, up +3.9% YoY (last week) (broad) (118.76 - 129.67) (Graph at Nominal Broad U.S. Dollar Index
  • Down -3.13 to 99.87 w/w, down -5.8% YoY (major currencies) (graph at link) (99.01- 109.66) (new 12 month low intraweek).

The neutral range for the US$ is between unchanged to 5% higher YoY. At 5% higher or more YoY, the strength suppresses exports and incentivizes imports, a negative for the economy. The trend in these measures had been from below 0 (thus positive) to neutral, to above 5% (negative) for 18 months, until the last couple of months. The broad US$ retreated under that threshold three weeks ago, and so is neutral, while the trade weighted US$ has been declining sharply, and this week was even more sharply lower YoY, so a positive.

Commodity prices

Bloomberg Commodity Index

  • Up +1.80 to 101.48 (93.30 - 107.71)
  • Down -1.4% YoY.

(Graph at https://www.marketwatch.com/investing/index/bcom?countrycode=xx.)

Bloomberg Industrial metals ETF (from Bloomberg) (graph at link)

  • 139.17, up +2.05 w/w (130.47 -173.08 )
  • Down -9.4% YoY.

Both of these measures improved earlier last year and industrial commodities surged to new 12 month highs in the autumn before backing off. The broad measure then turned back negative, and has since oscillated between that and neutral. Two months ago the industrial measure weakened to neutral for one week before rebounding, and then fell all the way to negative. The broad commodity index remained positive but last week declined sharply into negative territory, but rebounded into neutral range this week. By contrast, the industrial metals measure declined further this week including making a new 12 month low intraweek this week, and so is negative.

Stock prices S&P 500 (from CNBC) (graph at link)

  • Up +6.3% to 5391.50.

Despite the crash last week, we just had new all time high seven weeks ago. Because this indicator is very volatile, per my discipline remained neutral to guard against a V shaped rebound, which last week I wrote was explicitly “in case the tariffs are rescinded.” This week they were partially rescinded, except as to China, and there was a partial V shaped rebound, so they remain neutral.

Regional Fed New Orders Indexes

(*indicates report this week) (no reports this week)

  • Empire State down -26.3 to -14.9
  • Philly down -14.9 to +8.1
  • Richmond down -4 to -4
  • Kansas City down -5 to -12
  • *Dallas up +3.4 to -0.1
  • Month-over-month rolling average: up +1 to -4.

The regional average is more volatile than the ISM manufacturing index, but usually correctly forecasts its month-over-month direction. Since spring 2022, these gradually declined to neutral and then negative. The indexes varied sharply last year, but recently became more variable, first turning neutral and then one month+ ago to positive. This may have reflected manufacturers’ attempting to front-run new tariffs. Four of the five regions declined in March, and four of the five are in contraction.

As a special note, I am strongly considering adding the regional service indexes this month, to see if the consumer segment of the economy suddenly shrinks in as close to real time as reasonably available. The first of these will be reported this coming week.

Employment metrics

Initial jobless claims

  • 223,000, up +4,000 w/w, up +5.2% YoY
  • 4-week average 223,000, unchanged w/w, up +3.5% YoY.

(Graph at St. Louis FRED.)

Jobless claims were positive for most of 2024, except for hurricane-influenced weeks during the autumn. They subsequently returned to neutral, then briefly positive, before turning back to neutral. Should the four week average YoY climb above 10% higher, that would warrant a change of rating to negative.

Temporary staffing index (from the American Staffing Association) (graph at link)

  • Unchanged at 83 w/w
  • Down -7.6% YoY (low -13.7%- high -7.4%).

This series has been negative since early 2023. YoY comparisons continued to be negative throughout 2024. It remains frankly recessionary. I suspect this is a secular change and giving a false signal as a result. Relatively speaking, the YoY reading has been trending “less bad” for the past number of months, but not enough to change its negative rating.

Tax Withholding (from the Department of the Treasury)

  • $308.9 B for the last 20 reporting days vs. $289.8 B last year, up +$19.1 B or +6.6%.

These were very positive for almost all of 2024, and the trend has continued so far this year with the exception of two weeks - one of which was last week. This week YoY withholding bounced back to an average gain for this year to date.

Oil prices and usage (from the E.I.A.)

  • Oil down -$1.29 to $61.56 w/w, down -20.1% YoY ($62.85 - $86.59) (new 4 year low)
  • Gas prices up +$0.08 to $3.24 w/w, down -$0.35 YoY
  • Usage 4-week average down -2.8% YoY.

(Information can be found at “This Week In Petroleum” at the E.I.A.’s website.)

Oil prices first declined into the bottom 1/3rd of their 3 year range, and so turned positive several months ago. With the crash in commodity prices last week, they are now very positive.

Gas prices also recently made new 3 year lows, and so are also positive. Last year, mileage driven had been generally negative. It turned neutral and then positive earlier this year, but in March, it retreated to neutral for several weeks before turning negative last week.

Note: given this measure’s extreme volatility, I believe the best measure is against their 3 year average.

Bank lending rates

  • 4.37% Secured Overnight Financing Rate (SOFR), down -0.02% w/w.

The TED Spread has been discontinued, and as of last September, so was LIBOR. At the suggestion of a reader, I began to track the SOFR instead. Unfortunately, SOFR has only been in existence since 2018, so there is no track record has to how it might behave around normal recessions (vs. the pandemic). Over the past 5 years, it does appear to have matched the trend in LIBOR. Given the alternatives, I am now including it in my list of indicators in the conclusion.

Coincident indicators

St. Louis FRED Weekly Economic Index

  • Up +0.84 to 3.12 w/w (Low 1.48 May 4, 2024 - high 3.21 November 23, 2024).

This measure was in the neutral range during most of early 2024, but generally positive (above 2.00) since then, and - surprisingly - not only remained so this week, but came very close to setting a new 12 month high.

Restaurant reservations YoY (from Open Table) State of the Restaurant Industry | OpenTable

  • 7 day average down +4% YoY, vs. -4% one week ago.

Since 2023 this index has been very volatile, swinging between positive, neutral, and negative several times. In general this year the YoY trends have been improving, but with a great deal of noise. I suspect the change in the Easter weekend from late March last year to mid-April this year is affecting the last several weeks’ readings.

Consumer spending

  • Johnson Redbook up +7.2% YoY, 4 week average +5.7% (high 7.4% 12/6/24; low 4.8% February 1, 2025) United States Redbook Index.

The Redbook index has been positive since February last year. It has had very good readings for the past seven weeks. The link above goes to a 5 year graph to best show the comparison. I would take a decline below +3.0% YoY weekly a break in trend - which needless to say did not happen this week!

Consumer inflation by Truflation (Independent, economic & financial data in real time on-chain | Truflation)

  • Unchanged at +1.35% YoY (High 3.15% 4/27/24 - Low 1.06% 9/13/24).

This recent addition is a daily update to inflation, similar to the “billion prices project” of the last decade (which required a subscription). I have not added this to my list below of coincident or leading indicators, but needless to say it is an up-to-the-moment reading on this very important indicator. This series has been volatile, and the YoY comparisons had been turning higher since September, but fell during the past few weeks, then rose four weeks ago before declining last week.

Real Consumer Spending

  • Up +5.8% YoY (high - 5.8% 4/11/25; 12 month low -0.1% 2/17/24) (new 12 month high).

This metric premiered at the beginning of last year. One of my most important mantras is that consumption leads employment. Real retail sales have a long history of doing so, but are only reported on a monthly basis.

The weekly result is derived simply by subtracting YoY inflation as measured by Truflation from the YoY change in nominal consumer spending as measured by Redbook. While it will be somewhat noisy, it should anticipate changes in the monthly measures ahead of time. It backed off from the 12-month high it set in February, and then gradually rebounded, and made another 12 month high several months ago. After mainly weeks in the upper part of its 12-month range, it was tepid at the beginning of the year, before improving in the past six weeks, even making a new 12-month-high, then backing off. But this week it made another 12 month high. It is possible Easter spending is affecting this metric as well.

Transport

Railroads (from the AAR)

  • Carloads up +8.5% YoY
  • Intermodal units up +13.6% YoY
  • Total loads up +11.2% YoY.

(Graph at Railfax Report - North American Rail Freight Traffic Carloading Report.)

Shipping transport

  • Harpex up +24 to 2106 (new 12 month high)
  • Baltic Dry Index down -571 to 1269.

Rail data has been very volatile since early 2023, with lots of volatility from positive to negative and back again. For many weeks it was neutral, and it has continued to be noisy week to week, and mixed for many weeks before turning positive five weeks ago. The big increases in the past several weeks may reflect businesses attempting to get inventory before the new tariffs kick in.

Harpex was as high as nearly 4500 almost 2 years ago, but declined as low as 810 in December of last year before rising almost relentlessly since, setting repeated 12 month highs in the past three months. The usual interpretation for this is that demand is straining against supply, showing a strong global economy, and thus very positive.

Similarly, the BDI was in a generally declining trend throughout 2022 before bottoming at a three-year low of 530 in February 2023. The overall trend has been higher since then, rising to a high of 3346 last December, probably reflecting the Houthi attacks on shipping in the Red Sea. This year its low was 1309 in January, and its recent high was 2419 last March. It declined to the bottom 1/3rd of its one-year range during the rest of the year, but rebounded in the past eight weeks back into its middle range, and remained - just barely - in that range this week.

I am wary of reading too much into price indexes like this, since they are heavily influenced by supply (as in, a huge overbuilding of ships in the last decade) as well as demand.

Steel production ( American Iron and Steel Institute)

  • Down -2.4% w/w
  • Down -2.4% YoY.

This metric oscillated between positive and negative for most of 2024. In the past several months it was negative before rebounding to positive for three weeks. It then faded to neutral and back to negative for a month, before turning higher last week. This week it turned back negative.

Summary And Conclusion

Below are this week’s spreadsheets of the long leading, short leading, and coincident readings. Check marks indicate the present reading. If there has been a change this week, the prior reading is marked with an X:

.

This was - obviously - a whipsaw week, as the exogenous shock of (still) the highest tariffs since the infamous Smoot-Hawley tariffs of 1931 continued to reverberate, despite their intraweek re-jiggering.

The long leading indicators remain neutral, despite the sharp un-inversion of parts of the yield curve, including a sharp “bear steepening” at the long end. Unless something significant changes, corporate profits are likely to tip the balance within several weeks.

The short leading indicators, which were positive only two weeks ago, are now negative, as commodities, financial stress, and gas usage have all turned negative, while stocks remain neutral despite their gyrations.

The coincident indicators remained positive, mainly because consumer spending has held up. There may be evidence of frontrunning tariffs in the sharp increase in rail traffic.

I anticipate that sharp reversals (and re-reversals) in Washington will continue to drive the news cycle. The new 40 year low in consumer confidence is very concerning, but in the past it has usually taken several quarters for that to manifest in actual consumer and producer retrenchment. As usual, the hard data will tell, and the high frequency data will tell first.

data.title: What Moved Markets This Week
data.description:
Listen on the go! A daily podcast of Wall Street Breakfast will be available by 8:00 a.m. on Seeking Alpha, iTunes, Spotify.

Wall Street's benchmark S&P 500 index (SP500) on Friday posted its best weekly advance since early November 2023, after a rollercoaster few days.

Tariff developments continued to dominate headlines. The U.S. and China raised tariff rates against each other, further escalating a trade war between the world's two largest economies. However, market participants also received a major reprieve after U.S. President Donald Trump on Wednesday announced a 90-day pause on most reciprocal tariffs except for those against China.

Trump's move led to one of the most explosive single-session rallies in Wall Street history. The S&P (SP500) soared more than 9% to log its best intraday performance since the financial crisis in October 2008. Not to be outdone, the Nasdaq Composite (COMP:IND) skyrocketed more than 12% to notch its second-best day ever.

Traders also received some key inflation data this week. Both the headline consumer price and producer price indexes for March unexpectedly slipped.
